coredata.dc:title: A ratiometric fluorescent probe for a smartphone-based dual-emission carbon dot fluorescence sensor for the rapid detection of 5-HIAA
coredata.dc:creator: Han, Yuying
Hou, Xingyu
Li, Yali
Yang, Xiaopeng
Sun, Xue
Bian, Xinxin
Sun, Na
Lv, Yuguang
Ding, Lixin
Li, Ze
coredata.dc:description: This study utilized Panax notoginseng leaves carbon dots (PN-CDs) with red and blue fluorescence to detect 5-hydroxyindoleacetic acid (5-HIAA). It is a straightforward one-pot hydrothermal technique using Panax ginseng leaves to synthesize these CDs. Under 400 nm excitation, 5-HIAA decreases the fluorescence intensity at 670 nm while enhancing the fluorescence intensity at 420 nm. The fluorescence intensity ratio (F670/F420) exhibited a significant linear relationship over the 5-HIAA concentration range of 0–100 μM. Notably, smartphones could capture the observable color change of the PN-CDs under 365 nm UV exposure and perform RGB (red/green/blue) analysis for color quantification using the smartphone’s software. The detection limit for its fluorescence detection was determined to be 0.023 μM, whereas that of mobile phone detection was 0.36 μM. This study proposes a novel platform combining fluorescence sensing and cell phone technology, facilitating real-time analysis of 5-HIAA levels in human urine.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Panax notoginseng leaves carbon dots
5-Hydroxyindoleacetic acid
Ratiometric sensing
One-pot hydrothermal synthesis
Smartphone detection
Urine analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optimisation of CDs and detection conditions
Comparisons of the different synthesis methods

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Basic scientific research business of Heilongjiang Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This project was funded by Basic scientific research business of Heilongjiang Province (2023-KYYWF-0591).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of PN-CDs and the upper right corner is an HRTEM image with lattice fringes. (b) Size distribution of PN-CDs. (c) XRD pattern. (d) C1s, (e) O1s, and (f) N1s XPS spectra of PN-CDs. (g) FT-IR spectrum of PN-CDs. (h) The UV–vis spectrum, excitation, and fluorescence spectra of PN-CDs.

(a) Fluorescence lifetimes of PN-CDs with and without 5-HIAA. (b) UV–Vis of PN-CDs, 5-HIAA and PN-CDs + 5-HIAA. (c) Linearity of (F670/F420)0 / (F670/F420) with concentration of 5-HIAA. (d) Schematic representation of the fluorescence burst mechanism in PN-CDs.

(a) F670/F420 of PN-CDs with various interfering substances. (b) Fluorescent images of different substances. (c) F670/F420 of the PN-CDs-5-HIAA system before and after adding interferents. (d) Fluorescent images of the reaction with 5-HIAA.
Schematic of an PN-CDs-based ratio fluorescence sensor for 5-HIAA analysis.
Results of the determination of 5-HIAA in urine.

head.ce:title: A ratiometric fluorescent probe for a smartphone-based dual-emission carbon dot fluorescence sensor for the rapid detection of 5-HIAA

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Harbin Customs Technology Centre, Jiamusi 154007, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Harbin Customs Technology Centre, Jiamusi 154007, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Yuying Han and Xingyu Hou contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Schematic of an PN-CDs-based ratio fluorescence sensor for 5-HIAA analysis.

This study utilized Panax notoginseng leaves carbon dots (PN-CDs) with red and blue fluorescence to detect 5-hydroxyindoleacetic acid (5-HIAA). It is a straightforward one-pot hydrothermal technique using Panax ginseng leaves to synthesize these CDs. Under 400 nm excitation, 5-HIAA decreases the fluorescence intensity at 670 nm while enhancing the fluorescence intensity at 420 nm. The fluorescence intensity ratio (F670/F420) exhibited a significant linear relationship over the 5-HIAA concentration range of 0–100 μM. Notably, smartphones could capture the observable color change of the PN-CDs under 365 nm UV exposure and perform RGB (red/green/blue) analysis for color quantification using the smartphone’s software. The detection limit for its fluorescence detection was determined to be 0.023 μM, whereas that of mobile phone detection was 0.36 μM. This study proposes a novel platform combining fluorescence sensing and cell phone technology, facilitating real-time analysis of 5-HIAA levels in human urine.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Neurotransmitters and their metabolites in urine are typically recognized as critical biomarkers for cascading diseases and disorders that impact the human body. Ailments such as hypertension and hypotension, neuroblastoma, and even COVID-19 can be assessed by analyzing these biochemical substances in urine [1–3]. It is excreted via the urine, indicating the homeostatic condition of humans. For instance, the concentration of 5-hydroxyindoleacetic acid (5-HIAA) in cerebrospinal fluid may function as a predictor for suicide risk. Low levels of 5-HIAA are implicated in aggressive behavior, raised risk of suicide, and mood disorders in adult individuals, whereas elevated concentrations correlate with an increased risk of sudden infant death syndrome in infants under 12 months of age [4,5]. Elevated levels occur in association with the natural aging process, with its concentration in plasma considered to be a biomarker for appendiceal issues, carcinoid neoplasms, and colorectal cancer (8.87 μg·L-1 in patients as opposed to healthy individuals at 1.90 μg·L-1) [6,7]. Generally, the 5-HIAA level is a biomarker for tumors and various physical diseases in their early stages. Therefore, tracking this biomarker is crucial for clinical diagnosis [7,8].

The detection techniques for 5-HIAA consist of capillary electrophoresis, high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), fluorescence spectrophotometry, liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS), and electrochemical methods [9–13], among which fluorescence spectrophotometry extensively applied due to its simple operation and low equipment cost [14–16]. The rapid development of sensing platforms based on fluorescent nanomaterials coupled with probe and drug conjugation technologies has facilitated the extension of detection methods towards high sensitivity and diversification [17,18]. In recent years, to satisfy the demand for portable detection, smartphone sensing technology has become an emerging research direction: using the high sensitivity of the built-in camera of the cell phone to capture the color response or fluorescence signals, resolved digitally through the RGB model for red, green and blue channels (e.g. Color Grab or ImageJ software), and combined with the signal amplification of the fluorescent nanomaterials, to realize the analyte Semi-quantitative detection [19,20]. This technology breaks the dependence on traditional instruments, providing an efficient and user-friendly solution for exploiting clinical monitoring systems, especially in the biosensing and medical prompt detection fields, which shows a broad application potential [21,22].

In probing research a novel form of carbon nanomaterial known as carbon dots (CDs) has attracted much investigation focus due to its optical properties and potential applications in sensing, catalysis, and bio-imaging. CDs can be readily produced employing the hot solvent technique with biocompatible and cost-effective precursors, their emission properties influenced by size and excitation, hence exhibiting controllable and diverse emission properties [23–25]. Although CDs possess numerous strengths, most existing studies rely on single-wavelength measurements, the results of which suffer from factors like the environment, light source, probe concentration, and instrumentation. Researchers have integrated CDs with various fluorescent nanomaterials to fabricate ratiometric fluorescent sensors to overcome these limitations [26]. These ratiometric probes with dual emission peaks have dramatically boosted the efficiency and accuracy of biological analysis [27]. These self-calibrating ratiometric fluorescent probes demonstrate improved detection sensitivity. Up to this point, with most CDs-based ratiometric probes constructed by physical or chemical techniques using fluorescent materials, these techniques tend to be complex and time-consuming [26,28]. Consequently, there is a profound and ongoing interest in investigating a wider range of CDs that emit double fluorescence at a single excitation wavelength.

In the current study, we investigated novel Panax notoginseng leaves carbon dots (PN-CDs) for assaying 5-HIAA via employing a one-step hydrothermal approach with Panax notoginseng leaves and ethanol. As illustrated in Scheme 1 , when the wavelength was 400 nm, we observed two emission peaks at 420 nm and 670 nm. After introducing 5-HIAA, the fluorescent intensity at 670 nm was significantly quenched, while the fluorescent intensity at 420 nm significantly increased. For this experiment, a smartphone-based image acquisition system alongside an ultraviolet light source facilitated the excitation of fluorescence, determined the concentration of 5-HIAA by analyzing the RGB value of each image, and thus effectively merged a colorimetric probe with smartphone detection [27,28]. Furthermore, this study successfully analyzed 5-HIAA in urine samples using PN-CDs.

5-Hydroxyindole acetic acid (5-HIAA), phosphate buffer saline (PBS), aspartic acid (Asp), L-glutamic acid (L-Glu), methionine (Met), lysine (Lys), glucose (Glu), leucine (Leu) and urea were procured from Aladdin Ltd. In addition, anhydrous ethanol, Rhodamine B, zinc chloride, magnesium chloride, sodium chloride, potassium chloride, calcium chloride, sodium nitrate, sodium sulfate, and sodium carbonate were obtained from McLean Ltd.

Ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) spectra were obtained with a Shimadzu UV-2550 spectrophotometer from Kyoto, Japan. The relative fluorescence measurements were taken using a 970 CRT Fluorescence Spectrophotometer, which featured excitation and emission slits of 5.0 and 10.0 nm, respectively (Sanke Instrument Co., Ltd, Shanghai, China). For transmission electron microscopy (TEM) imaging, a Tecnai G2 F30 S-Twin microscope (FEI, Hillsboro, OR, USA) was utilized. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded using an IRTracer-100, another spectrophotometer provided by Shimadzu. Chemical composition assessments were conducted via X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Phi V5000). The structures of the samples were characterized utilizing an X-ray diffractometer (XRD, XRD-6000, Shimadzu, Tokyo, Japan). Finally, the pH levels of the solutions were assessed using a PHS-3F pH meter from Shanghai Yidian Scientific Instrument Co., Ltd, China.

In the process of synthesizing PN-CDs, fresh leaves of Panax notoginseng were gathered, cleaned to eliminate surface particles, dried in an oven, and then ground into a fine powder and sieved (35 mesh). Weigh 3 g of the powdered material and combined it with 100 mL anhydrous ethanol and mix for 12 h. Afterward, the mixture was filtered through filter paper, resulting in a clarified green solution. This solution was then placed in a 25 mL PTFE reactor lined with a protective layer and heated in an oven at 150 ℃ to facilitate the reaction, which yielded Panax notoginseng leaves exhibiting recessive signals. Following this, the PN-CDs were subjected to centrifugation at 5000 rpm to separate insoluble substances, followed by filtration and purification using a microporous membrane filter with a pore diameter of 0.22 μm. Ultimately, employ a dialysis bag designed for a molecular weight cutoff of 1000 Da to dialyze the resultant solution for 24 h, after which it was dried and stored at 4 ℃ for later use and analysis (Fig. S1).

The assessment of 5-HIAA utilizing PN-CDs was conducted by the following methodology. Initially, the fluorescent spectra were acquired within the wavelength range of 320–750 nm, with an excitation wavelength of 400 nm. To determine the concentration of 5-HIAA, 1.0 mL solution of PBS (pH 5.0, 10 mM) was used in conjunction with PN-CDs. Different concentrations of 5-HIAA were introduced, and thoroughly mixed, and the resulting fluorescence variations were documented. This process was repeated at least three times to ensure the reliability of the data.

The procedure for determining 5-HIAA with a mobile phone was conducted in the following manner. A 10 μL aliquot of a standard 5-HIAA solution with varying concentrations was added drop by drop to the system. Fluorescence images were then taken using a mobile phone under a 365 nm UV lamp after a duration of 90 s. The RGB valued from these images were separated into three channels-red (R), green (G), and blue (B) and subsequently analyzed through a color recognition application.

The analysis of urine samples was conducted to assess the efficacy of the proposed methodology concerning 5-HIAA. These samples were provided by healthy individuals. To guarantee accurate data, careful measures were implemented during the collection process, such as refraining from consuming caffeinated drinks (like tea and coffee), specific medications, and foods high in serotonin (including bananas, pineapples, and plums) for a duration of 48 h throughout the collection period. Following collection, the urine samples were subjected to centrifugation at 5000 rpm to eliminate large proteins and any insoluble components. The resulting supernatant was then isolated and filtered using a 0.22 μm microporous membrane filter to enhance purification. Finally, the urine samples were diluted by a factor of 20 to 30, and the concentration of 5-HIAA was quantified.

The PN-CDs microstructure and morphology were analyzed with the help of TEM. Fig. 1 a illustrated that the PN-CDs were evenly dispersed and displayed a consistent spherical form. A lattice spacing of 0.20 nm was observed in the PN-CDs. Additionally, it demonstrated a particle size distribution between 1.35 and 4.85 nm, with an average measurement of 1.96 nm, as depicted in Fig. 1b. The XRD spectrum (Fig. 1c) of the PN-CDs illustrated broad peaks, which were attributed to the substantial disorder among the carbon atoms. These broad diffraction peaks likely arised from weak interactions among layers in the graphite-like nanostructures.

The XPS served as a significant technique for analyzing the elemental composition and surface characteristics of PN-CDs. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s (Fig. 1d) presented four major peaks at 284.8 eV (C-C/C=C), 286.4 eV (C-O), 287 eV (C=N), and 288.9 eV (C=O). The O1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 1e) for the PN-CDs revealed two prominent peaks at 532.5 eV (CO) and 532.9 eV (C-O). Additionally, the N1s spectrum (Fig. 1f) displayed six distinct peaks at 397.3 eV (Nitride), 398.2 eV (Pyridinic), 399 eV (−C=NH), 399.9 eV (NH2), 403.8 eV (Pyridinic-oxide), and 404.6 eV (NO2). The chemical makeup of the PN-CDs was analyzed via Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy, as depicted in Fig. 1g. Distinctive peaks in the range of 1630–1755 cm−1 were detected, which corresponded to the stretching vibrations of C=C, C=O, and C=N bonds, suggesting the existence of aromatic rings and extensive conjugated systems. Furthermore, the PN-CDs exhibited peaks ranging from 2775 to 3128 cm−1 was linked to the vibrations of –CH2–, which indicated hydrophobic elongated alkyl chains on the PN-CDs surface. In addition, the detection of a polymer shell comprising alkyl chains was supported by peaks between 600 and 768 cm−1, attributed to C-H out-of-plane bending vibrations. A peak at 1437 cm−1 was also noted for C-N stretching vibrations, while characteristic peaks in the range of 946–1157 cm−1 were associated with C-O-C stretching vibrations, probably connected to the ester group of chlorophyll. As a result, the synthesized dual-emission PN-CDs were sourced from both the internal carbon cores and the surface chlorophyll.

Natural biomass was recognized as a cost-effective and renewable resource for carbon and nitrogen used in the synthesis of PN-CDs. Nonetheless, the hydrothermal processing of biomass usually resulted in products that exhibited solely blue emission [29].High concentrations of chlorophyll present in biomass could act as precursors, producing CDs enriched with chlorophyll on their surfaces, which led to red emission [30].The optical characteristics of the PN-CDs were illustrated in Fig. 1h, where the UV–vis spectrum displayed three distinct absorption peaks located at 275, 405, and 650 nm. The absorption peak observed at 275 nm was linked to the π-π* transition of CN, CO, or CC bonds within the nanocarbon structure. The remaining peaks, positioned at 405 nm and 650 nm, were possibly attributable to the n-π* transition of CO and CN bonds found in chlorophyll [31]. For fluorescence analysis, the PN-CDs exhibited dual emission peaks at 420 and 670 nm under 400 nm excitation. It showed an excitation peak at 310 nm, and the excitation-dependent behaviour may be due to internal carbon nuclei with different particle sizes and diverse surface states. In contrast, it indicated three separate excitation peaks at 410, 600 and 650 nm, showing excitation-independent behaviour, probably due to the chlorophyll on its surface [31]. Lastly, the relative fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of the PN-CDs was approximately 31 % in Fig. S2.

Under optimal experimental conditions (Fig. S3a), the performance of the PN-CDs sensor was thoroughly evaluated. The fluorescence intensity ratio of the PN-CDs exhibited a clear correlation with increasing concentrations of 5-HIAA, as shown in Fig. S3b. A linear relationship was established over the concentration range of 2.0 to 100.0 μM, represented by the equationy = 0.0075x + 0.056, with an excellent coefficient of determination (R2 = 0.9973). The limit of detection (LOD) for 5-HIAA was calculated to be 0.023 μM using the formula3σ/S, whereσrepresented the standard deviation of blank measurements, andSwas the slope of the linear equation. This calculation was repeated across three trials, confirming the high sensitivity and reliability of the PN-CDs sensor for detecting low concentrations of 5-HIAA.

To explore the sensing mechanism of PN-CDs for 5-HIAA, a comprehensive series of experiments were conducted. The UV–Vis of 5-HIAA, alongside the fluorescence spectra of the CDs, were illustrated in Fig. 2 b. Notably, these spectra revealed no overlap between the absorption spectrum of 5-HIAA and the excitation and emission spectra of the CDs. This indicated that the interaction between the CDs and the analyte cannot be attributed to phenomena such as the inner filter effect (IFE) or Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) [32]. Additionally, the average fluorescence lifetime of the PN-CDs solution in the presence of the analyte (τ1) was measured at 2.0 ns, while in its absence (τ0), the lifetime was recorded at 2.1 ns (Fig. 2a). Since fluorescence lifetime remained unaffected by the introduction of the compound, and the dynamic quenching effect (DQE) resulted in variations in fluorescence lifetime while static quenching effect (SQE) did not, we deduced that the interaction was indicative of a static quenching mechanism. Our hypothesis was further substantiated by the UV–Vis absorption spectra of the PN-CDs, 5-HIAA, and the PN-CDs + 5-HIAA. As illustrated in Fig. 2b, the significant shifted in absorption peaks at 232 and 270 nm following the addition of 5-HIAA to the PN-CDs solution (red) point toward the establishment of a ground-state complex within the PN-CDs + 5-HIAA system. Moreover, both SQE and DQE adhere to the Stern–Volmer equation (1):
(1)
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K
SV
Q

The fluorescence intensities of the PN-CDs in the absence and presence of 5-HIAA were denoted as (F670/F420)0 and F670/F420, respectively. The Stern-Volmer constant was represented by KSV, while [Q] indicated the concentration of 5-HIAA. A pronounced linear relationship was observed between the ratio (F670/F420)0 / (F670/F420) and the concentration of 5-HIAA (Fig. 2c), with Ksv calculated to be 2.76 × 105 L·mol−1 and an R2 value of 0.9948. To further understand the quenching mechanism, the quenching rate constant (Kq) could be determined using the following equation (2):
(2)
K
SV
=
K
q τ 0

The calculatedKqvalue was3.47 × 1014 L·mol−1·s−1, which was significantly higher than the dynamic quenching constant (2 × 1010 L·mol−1·s−1). This indicated that the interaction with 5-HIAA was likely driven by SQE. At 670 nm, the PN-CDs contained porphyrin structures that formed non-covalent complexes with 5-HIAA via π-π stacking, forming a blunted fluorescence burst of non-fluorescent ground state complexes in the surface state. Meanwhile, at 420 nm, the –NH2 and –COOH of 5-HIAA binded to the PN-CDs to form a reduced, non-radiative complex. The nuclear quantum confinement effect was enhanced by short-wave emission and hydrogen bond formation, thus constituting a dual-response ratiometric fluorescence [33,34]. A schematic representation of the fluorescence mechanism was shown in Fig. 2d. Under UV irradiation, electrons absorbed energy and transited from the ground state to the excited state. Additionally, the mechanism confirmed that the ratiometric fluorescence sensor was successfully synthesized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimisation of CDs and detection conditions

ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties and stability of PN-CDs were critical factors influencing their performance in sensing applications. To evaluate these attributes, the effects of synthesis temperature, reaction duration, pH, storage conditions, and UV irradiation time were systematically investigated (Fig. S4). The results revealed that the fluorescence intensity of the PN-CDs at 420 and 670 nm remained highly stable under a range of conditions. Specifically, when synthesized at 150 °C for 4 h, stored in a PBS solution at pH 4 for 14 d, and exposed to 365 nm UV irradiation for 180 min, only minimal change in fluorescence intensity was observed. These findings underscored the exceptional optical stability and robustness of the synthesized PN-CDs, making them highly suitable for practical sensing applications under various environmental and operational conditions.

To optimize the response to 5-HIAA, key assay parameters were systematically evaluated, including the concentration of PN-CDs, pH, and reaction temperature (Fig. S5). The results revealed that the fluorescence bursting efficiency was maximized under specific conditions: a PN-CDs concentration ratio of 1:15, pH = 5, and a reaction temperature of 25°C. Furthermore, the reaction reached equilibrium within 90 s of combining PN-CDs with 5-HIAA, demonstrating a rapid and efficient response. These findings highlighted the importance of precise parameter optimization to enhance the sensitivity and reliability of the sensing platform, ensuring its effectiveness for practical applications.

Selectivity was a crucial factor when assessing the feasibility of the PN-CDs. To evaluate this property, various potential interfering substances were introduced into the solution, including 5-HIAA, Asp, Leu, Lys, Met, Urea, Glu, K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Cl-, CO3 2–, NO3 –, and SO4 2-. As shown in Fig. 3 a, only 5-HIAA significantly quenched the fluorescence of the PN-CDs, while the other substances had little impact on the system. Fluorescent images of different interfering substances were presented in Fig. 3b. When the PN-CDs + 5-HIAA sensing system was exposed to competing substances, even when their concentration was three times higher than that of 5-HIAA, the F670/F420 ratio showed no notable variation (Fig. 3c). Fluorescent images of the reaction with 5-HIAA were shown in Fig. 3d. Thus, the prepared PN-CDs could effectively serve as a ratiometric fluorescence probe for detecting 5-HIAA.

The solution's color resulting from the reaction with 5-HIAA was captured using a smartphone camera (Fig. S6a). As 5-HIAA interacted with the system, the color gradually transited from red to light pink, then to light purple, and ultimately to blue. The RGB value of the resulting color was extracted via the smartphone's color recognition tool, leading to the establishment of a B/R analysis model correlated with the analyte concentration (Fig. S6b). Data analysis revealed a strong linear relationship between the concentration and the B/R value within the range of 2–80 μM (R2 = 0.9937), with a LOD of 0.36 μM. This suggested that smartphones could effectively recognize the fluorescence color of the ratio probe solution, enabling rapid quantitative analysis. To enhance portability, ratiometric fluorescent test strips were developed for on-site detection. The RGB values of the test strips were assessed after the reaction using a smartphone, which demonstrated that the combination of these strips and smartphone-based analysis allowed for quick and visual quantitative detection of 5-HIAA in field applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparisons of the different synthesis methods
ce:section.ce:para: The present study exploited a cost-effective fluorescence mobile phone (FM) sensor by merging ratiometric fluorescence detection with a smartphone platform. Existing 5-HIAA detection methods (Table S1) were restricted to single-target analysis and bulky instruments, while our strategy realizes simultaneous multi-target detection utilizing dual-wavelength ratiometric fluorescence. The system employed smartphone imaging functionality and fluorescence detection to reduce instrument complexity, as well as maintaining high sensitivity across a wide linear range. Optimized signal processing algorithms ensured stable performance with relative standard deviations (RSD) of 2.3–4.8 % and 2.3–3.0 %, respectively. The platform alleviated ambient light interference and probes inhomogeneity inherent to single-wavelength approaches by adopting ratiometric analysis. This work offered a portable, high-precision sensing solution for point-of-care testing and field monitoring.

In addition, the probes prepared in this work offered the following merits: (1) Adoption of a one-step synthesis method. (2) Selection of the green Panax notoginseng leaf as the raw material. (3) The construction of a ratiometric fluorescence sensor with strong anti-interference ability. (4) High detection sensitivity and selectivity. (5) Portable visualization of cellular phone inspection achieved.

To evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed method, we employed dual-emission CDs to detect 5-HIAA in urine samples obtained from humans. The urine sample was diluted, and the resultant diluted solution was used for testing purposes. Various concentrations of 5-HIAA (10, 20, and 30 μM) were incorporated into the samples, with the analytical results presented in Table 1 . The fluorescence analysis recoveries for all three concentrations ranged from 96.70 % to 105.58 %, showing an RSD (n = 3) between 2.3 % and 4.8 %. Meanwhile, the RGB analysis recoveries were consistently between 91.00 % and 101.90 %, with an RSD (n = 3) ranging from 2.3 % to 3.0 %. These findings demonstrated that the utilization of dual-emission CDs was both practical and precise for detecting 5-HIAA in human urine samples.

In summary, this study not only proposes a one-step synthesis technique for dual-emission PN-CDs but also introduces a simple, sensitive, efficient, and highly selective approach for detecting 5-HIAA in complicated real-world samples. The platform accomplishes real-time on-site detection by integrating the probe with a smartphone-based RGB analysis tool, achieving recoveries of 96.70 % to 105.58 % for fluorescence detection and 91.00 % to 101.90 % for RGB analysis. This innovative strategy underscores the potential of dual-emission PN-CDs for the detection of various analytes in environmental and healthcare applications.

Yuying Han: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Xingyu Hou: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Conceptualization. Yali Li: Project administration, Formal analysis. Xiaopeng Yang: Visualization, Investigation, Data curation. Xue Sun: Resources, Methodology. Xinxin Bian: Methodology, Formal analysis. Na Sun: Validation, Methodology, Conceptualization. Yuguang Lv: Software, Resources, Methodology. Lixin Ding: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Conceptualization. Ze Li: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was funded by Basic scientific research business of Heilongjiang Province (2023-KYYWF-0591).
